items.title: Arteritic vs non-arteritic
items.contentHtml:
Arteritic vs non-arteritic

  • arteritic AION (AION-A): secondary to giant cell arteritis (GCA); inflammatory occlusion of short posterior ciliary arteries.
  • Non-arteritic AION (AION-NA): infarction of the optic nerve due to hypoperfusion in a disc with drusen or small disc and absent excavation (disc at risk).
  • Arteritic has a worse visual prognosis; non-arteritic usually stabilizes without treatment.
  • Key fact: ESR >100 mm/h suggests arteritic; CRP also elevated (>2.5 mg/dL).

items.title: Association with ACG
items.contentHtml:
Association with giant cell arteritis

  • Arteritic AION is the main ocular complication of giant cell arteritis (GCA).
  • Granulomatous inflammation of the ophthalmic artery and ciliary branches produces ischemia of the optic nerve.
  • ACR 1990 criteria for GCA: age ≥50 years, ESR ≥50 mm/h, recent onset headache, temporal artery hypersensitivity, positive temporal artery biopsy (≥3 criteria: sensitivity 93.5%).
  • Examination trap: arteritic AION can be the first manifestation of GCA even without headache.

items.title: Sudden painless visual loss
items.contentHtml:
Sudden painless visual loss

  • Sudden painless visual loss is the typical presentation of AION (arteritic and non-arteritic).
  • In non-arteritic AION it is usually noticed upon awakening (nocturnal hypotension).
  • Arteritic AION can occur. be accompanied by systemic symptoms such as headache, jaw claudication or polymyalgia rheumatica.
  • Main differential diagnosis: optic neuritis (pain with eye movements, progressive loss in hours/days, frequent recovery).

items.title: Relative afferent pupillary defect
items.contentHtml:
Relative afferent pupillary defect

  • The relative afferent pupillary defect (RPD) is an objective sign of unilateral optic nerve damage.
  • It is evaluated with the swinging flashlight test: decreased pupillary response when illuminating the affected eye, with paradoxical dilation.
  • In AION the DPAR is positive except if there is symmetrical bilateral involvement (rare).
  • The presence of DPAR helps to differentiate retinal causes of visual loss (for example central retinal artery occlusion, which also gives DPAR, but the fundus is different).

items.foco: Optic disc edema in acute phase
items.title: Acute disc edema
items.contentHtml:
Optic disc edema in the acute phase

  • In the acute phase of AION optic disc edema (papilledema) is observed in the fundus of the eye.
  • In arteritic AION the edema is usually paler (pale disc edema) and may be associated with cotton-like exudates.
  • In Non-arteritic AION the edema is more hyperemic and there are frequently flaming peripapillary hemorrhages.
  • After weeks, the edema resolves and leaves optic atrophy (pale disc and increased cupping).

items.title: Visual field: lower altitude defect
items.contentHtml:
Visual field: lower altitudinal defect

  • The classic field defect of AION is the inferior altitudinal defect (loss of the lower half of the visual field).
  • It is more common in non-arteritic AION; Higher altitudinal scotomas or arcuate defects may also be seen.
  • The defect respects the horizontal midline (altitudinal) unlike hemianopsias.
  • Optic neuritis typically produces central or centrocecal scotoma, not altitudinal.

items.title: Elevated ESR and CRP
items.contentHtml:
ESR and CRP elevated in arteritic form

  • In arteritic AION due to giant cell arteritis, the ESR is usually markedly elevated (>100 mm/h).
  • The CRP is also elevated (>2.5 mg/dL); the combination of elevated ESR + elevated CRP has sensitivity >97% for GCA.
  • In non-arteritic AION the acute phase reactants are normal.
  • Trap: up to 4% of GCA confirmed by biopsy have normal ESR; In these cases CRP may be the only elevated marker.

items.title: Arteritic AION: emergency with steroids
items.contentHtml:
Arteritic AION: emergency with steroids

  • The arteritic AION is a medical emergency; Treatment is intravenous methylprednisolone 1 g/day for 3-5 days, followed by oral prednisone 1 mg/kg/day.
  • The objective is to prevent visual loss of the contralateral eye (risk >50% without treatment).
  • Initiate steroids immediately in case of clinical suspicion, without waiting for temporal artery biopsy (this can be performed within 7-14 days).
  • The treatment is: Non-arteritic AION has no proven effective treatment; steroids are not used.

items.title: Non-arteritic AION: no treatment
items.contentHtml:
Non-arteritic AION: no effective treatment

  • Non-arteritic AION lacks therapy that modifies the visual prognosis; management is preventive and of risk factors.
  • Studies such as the Ischemic Optic Neuropathy Decompression Trial (IONDT) demonstrated that decompression of the optic nerve is harmful.
  • There is no benefit with anticoagulants, antiplatelets or steroids (the latter can worsen risk factors such as diabetes or HBP).
  • Control of hypertension is recommended, diabetes mellitus, dyslipidemia and avoiding nocturnal hypotension.

items.foco: Risk factor: nocturnal high blood pressure
items.title: Nighttime hypertension as a risk factor
items.contentHtml:
Risk factor: nocturnal arterial hypertension

  • Nocturnal arterial hypertension (lack of nocturnal decrease in blood pressure, non-dipper pattern) is an independent risk factor for non-arteritic AION.
  • Sustained elevation of pressure during the night can compromise optic nerve perfusion in a disc with disk at risk.
  • It is documented by 24-hour ambulatory blood pressure monitoring (ABPM).
  • Treatment should include antihypertensives that preserve the physiological nocturnal decrease (for example avoid nocturnal doses of diuretics).

items.title: Avoid nocturnal hypotension
items.contentHtml:
Non-arteritic AION: avoid nocturnal hypotension

  • Nocturnal hypotension (excessive drop in blood pressure during sleep) can precipitate Non-arteritic AION in patients with at-risk optic disc.
  • Special care with long-acting antihypertensive drugs taken at bedtime, which can cause hypoperfusion of the optic nerve.
  • It is recommended to avoid nocturnal hypotension adjusting medication schedules (prefer morning doses) and monitoring nocturnal blood pressure.
  • Other factors: obstructive sleep apnea (cause of hypoxia and nocturnal hypotension) should be investigated and treated.

items.title: Differential diagnosis: optic neuritis
items.contentHtml:
Differential diagnosis with optic neuritis

  • Optic neuritis: pain with eye movements (95%), subacute visual loss in days, central scotoma, spontaneous recovery in weeks.
  • AION: painless, sudden visual loss (seconds to hours), altitude defect, acute disc edema, positive DPAR.
  • The neuritis optics typically affects young adults (20-40 years) and is associated with multiple sclerosis; AION in people over 50 years of age with vascular risk factors.
  • Brain magnetic resonance imaging with gadolinium in optic neuritis may show enhancement of the optic nerve; in NOIA there is no significant enhancement.
